entry.jurisdiction: Stagstrup Sogn, Vestervig, Thisted Amt, Denmark
entry.rationale: The baptismal register (kirkebog) for Stagstrup Sogn would contain the entry for Mikkel's baptism on or around 20 August 1779, recording his father's full name and often occupation and residence. Searching 'Denmark, Church Records, 1484–1941' (collection 2078555) — the largest indexed Danish church record collection on FamilySearch — is the highest-probability first step to directly identify the father. The patronymic 'Nielsen' implies the father's given name was Niels; the farm name 'Blyeberg' helps narrow to the specific household.

entry.jurisdiction: Stagstrup Sogn, Vestervig, Thisted Amt, Denmark
entry.rationale: Fallback baptism search in 'Denmark, Baptisms, 1618–1923' (collection 1778463), a separately indexed FamilySearch collection covering Danish baptisms. Indexing methodology differs from collection 2078555, so the same record may be retrievable through this alternate index when the primary search fails.

entry.jurisdiction: Stagstrup Sogn, Hassing, Thisted Amt, Denmark
entry.rationale: The 1787 Danish census (the first national census) would show Mikkel, aged approximately 7–8, in his father's household. The father would be listed as head of household with his full name, age, and occupation. Available through 'Denmark, Church Records, 1484–1941' and also indexed at Ancestry and DanishFamilySearch. This provides the father's identity in a second independent record type.

Denmark's military conscription rolls (lægdsruller) are direct parentage evidence: boys were enrolled under their father's name, with the father's name written directly above the son's entry. Mikkel (born 1779) would appear in the 1789–1793 rolls at ages 10–14. FamilySearch collection 2243379 ('Denmark, Military Conscription Rolls, 1789–1793') is indexed and covers Thisted Amt. This is independent direct evidence of who Mikkel's father was, and notably records the father's death if it occurred during the roll period.

entry.jurisdiction: Stagstrup Sogn, Vestervig, Thisted Amt, Denmark
entry.rationale: A dedicated search for the marriage of Mikkel's parents in 'Denmark, Marriages, 1635–1916' (collection 1520592). For this parentage question, the parents' marriage record is a distinct and critical source: it confirms the couple as a unit, supplies the mother's maiden name (which census records typically omit), and — by its date relative to Mikkel's 1779 birth — provides direct evidence of paternity. Searching for marriages of 'Niels' in Stagstrup/Vestervig ca. 1750–1779.

Search 'Denmark, Probate Indexes, 1674–1851' (collection 2026233) for an estate record (skifteprotokol) naming the father. If the father died, a probate record would list surviving heirs including Mikkel by name, confirming the parent-child relationship. The volume survey identified skifteprotokoller for Stagstrup, Vestervig (1773–1777) and adjacent Hurup, Vestervig (1779–1803) in image group 007141151 — useful as a browse fallback if indexed search is incomplete.

A broad search for surname Blyeberg in Danish census records returned only 1845–1906 results. Incidentally noted: Mikkel Nielsen Blaberg (born 1779, Stagstrup, Hassing, Thisted) appears as a widower in the Denmark Census 1860 (ark:/61903/1:1:QLDZ-7H51), confirming continued Stagstrup residence — useful corroboration for identity but not paternity. 1787 census evidence unavailable via indexed search; original images would require a volume browse.

Bride surname Michelsdatter confirms why the son was named Mikkel/Michel (named for maternal grandfather per Danish custom). Clear strong match for the parents' marriage record sought by pli_005.

2. MARRIAGE RECORD (1778, corroborating indirect evidence). The Denmark Marriages 1635–1916 collection (ark:/61903/1:1:FVR3-287) records the marriage of Niels Madsen Blyeberg to Inger Michelsdatter on 3 January 1778 at Stagstrup, Thisted, Denmark. This marriage occurred 18 months before Mikkel’s baptism, entirely consistent with Niels being the father. The bride’s surname Michelsdatter (daughter of Michel) explains why the son was named Mikkel — the Danish custom of naming children after grandparents. This record independently establishes that a Niels Blyeberg was present in Stagstrup parish and married to Mikkel’s mother.

3. PROBATE INDEX (1803, corroborating evidence). The Denmark Probate Indexes 1674–1851 (ark:/61903/1:1:Q2WL-6QST) records a probate proceeding in 1803 for Niels Madsen Bliberg of 'Sundy Stagstrup,' with wife Inger Mikkelsdatter. The name, patronymic, and residence all match the marriage record. The wife’s surname variant (Mikkelsdatter vs. Michelsdatter) is a routine Danish orthographic variant of the same name.

The Denmark Burials 1640–1917 index (ark:/61903/1:1:FVRC-V2F) for the burial of Mikkel Nielsen Bliberg on 18 July 1862 at Stagstrup includes a ParentChild relationship listing 'Niels Bliberg' (ark:/61903/1:1:FVRC-V2N) as Mikkel’s father. Although this record post-dates Niels’s death by 60 years and the informant is unknown, it independently records the same parentage conclusion reached by the 1779 baptism.

entry.narrative_markdown:
## Father of Mikkel Nielsen Blyeberg (baptized 20 August 1779, Stagstrup, Thisted Amt, Denmark)

**Conclusion (Probable):** The father of Mikkel Nielsen Blyeberg was **Niels Blyeberg**, whose full name was **Niels Madsen Blyeberg**. The conclusion rests on strong preponderance from four independent record types; it is classified *probable* rather than *proved* because all searched sources are derivative databases and original parish register images were not accessed to confirm the indexed entries.

### Evidence

**1. Baptismal record — direct evidence (primary information)**
The “Denmark Baptisms, 1618–1923” database on FamilySearch (derivative source, col. 1778463) records the christening of *Michel* on 20 August 1779 at Stagstrup, Thisted, Denmark, naming *Niels Blyeberg* as father (ark:/61903/1:1:H26C-HMW2).¹ The date and place exactly match the research subject’s known birth data. The name *Michel* is the standard 18th-century Latin/German form of *Mikkel* used in Danish Lutheran parish registers. The recording clergyman, present as a witness at the baptism, provided primary information for the baptism date and the father’s name. This is the most direct evidence available.

**2. Marriage record — indirect corroboration (primary information)**
The “Denmark, Marriages, 1635–1916” database (derivative, col. 1520592) records the marriage of *Niels Madsen Blyeberg* to *Inger Michelsdatter* on 3 January 1778 at Stagstrup, Thisted (ark:/61903/1:1:FVR3-287).² This marriage occurred eighteen months before Mikkel’s baptism, placing Niels Blyeberg in Stagstrup parish as the husband of Mikkel’s mother well before the child’s birth. The bride’s surname *Michelsdatter* (daughter of Michel) explains the son’s given name Mikkel — the Danish convention of naming children for grandparents. The patronymic *Madsen* (son of Mads) resolves Niels’s full name. Both parties to the marriage provided primary information about their own identities.

**3. Probate index — corroborating evidence (indeterminate information)**
The “Denmark, Probate Indexes, 1674–1851” database (derivative, col. 2026233) records a probate proceeding in 1803 for *Niels Madsen Bliberg*, residence “Sundy Stagstrup,” with widow *Inger Mikkelsdatter* (ark:/61903/1:1:Q2WL-6QST).³ The name (including patronymic Madsen), farm surname (Bliberg = Blyeberg), and wife’s name all match the 1778 marriage record, corroborating that this is the same individual. The informant for the probate’s name fields is unknown, rendering information quality indeterminate. The probate’s 1803 date indicates Niels died in or around 1802–1803.

**4. Burial record of Mikkel — corroborating direct evidence (indeterminate information)**
The “Denmark Burials, 1640–1917” database (derivative, col. 1520593) records the burial of *Mikkel Nielsen Bliberg* on 18 July 1862 at Stagstrup, Thisted, with *Niels Bliberg* listed in a ParentChild relationship as Mikkel’s father (ark:/61903/1:1:FVRC-V2F; father: FVRC-V2N).⁴ Although this record post-dates Niels’s death by sixty years and the informant is unknown (indeterminate quality), it independently records the same parentage stated in the 1779 baptism.

### Assessment and Limitations

All four sources consistently name Niels Blyeberg (Niels Madsen Blyeberg) as Mikkel’s father or as the husband of Mikkel’s mother in Stagstrup. No source names a different father. The sources span four independent record types (baptism, marriage, probate, burial) with distinct informants and institutional origins. Name variants (Blyeberg/Bliberg; Michelsdatter/Mikkelsdatter) are routine Danish orthographic equivalents.

The conclusion is classified *probable* because all sources are derivative indexes; original Stagstrup parish register images for 1750–1815 were not found in FamilySearch’s volume search, and the 1787 census and Hassing herred lægdsruller are not in accessible indexed collections. Access to the original baptismal register entry and its surrounding page would advance the tier to *proved*.

### Footnotes
¹ “Denmark Baptisms, 1618–1923,” database, FamilySearch (https://familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:2:WDTL-3PT2 : accessed 9 July 2026), entry for Michel, baptized 20 August 1779, Stagstrup, Thisted, Denmark.
² “Denmark, Marriages, 1635–1916,” database, FamilySearch (https://familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:2:MGY3-GQN : accessed 9 July 2026), entry for Niels Madsen Blyeberg and Inger Michelsdatter, married 3 January 1778, Stagstrup, Thisted, Denmark.
³ “Denmark, Probate Indexes, 1674–1851,” database, FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:2:QL3F-FM7C : accessed 9 July 2026), entry for Niels Madsen Bliberg and Inger Mikkelsdatter, 1803; residence Sundy Stagstrup.
⁴ “Denmark Burials, 1640–1917,” database, FamilySearch (https://familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:2:1W4H-V3Z : 20 February 2020), entry for Mikkel Nielsen Bliberg, buried 18 July 1862, Stagstrup, Thisted, Denmark.

entry.narrative_markdown:
## Death Date of Niels Madsen Blyeberg (father of Mikkel Nielsen Blyeberg)

**Conclusion (Probable):** Niels Madsen Blyeberg, father of Mikkel Nielsen Blyeberg, was buried on **20 December 1802** at Stagstrup, Thisted, Denmark, and died on or shortly before that date. The conclusion is *probable* rather than *proved* because the source is a derivative index; original parish burial register images for Stagstrup were not accessed.

The “Denmark Burials, 1640–1917” database on FamilySearch (derivative, col. 1520593) records the burial of *Niels Madsen Blyeberg* on 20 December 1802 at Stagstrup, Thisted, Denmark, with birth year indexed as 1743 (ark:/61903/1:1:FVRL-8PG).¹ The burial date is primary information recorded by the officiating parish clergyman, who was present as a witness at the interment. In rural Denmark in 1802, civil death registration had not yet been established; the burial register is the authoritative death record for this era. Danish Lutheran practice typically saw burial within two to seven days of death; the burial date of 20 December 1802 is therefore the best available evidence for the approximate date of Niels’s death.

This date is corroborated by the “Denmark, Probate Indexes, 1674–1851” (derivative, col. 2026233), which records a probate proceeding opened in 1803 for *Niels Madsen Bliberg* of Stagstrup with widow *Inger Mikkelsdatter* (ark:/61903/1:1:Q2WL-6QST).² A probate opened in 1803 is consistent with a death in late 1802. The two sources are from independent record types with different institutional origins and agree on the approximate death date.

No source places Niels Blyeberg alive after December 1802. The original burial register image was not accessed (not available via FamilySearch volume search), but the indexed entry provides a specific date. Access to the original image would advance the conclusion to *proved*.

### Footnotes
¹ “Denmark Burials, 1640–1917,” database, FamilySearch (https://familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:2:1W4H-RT2 : 20 February 2020), entry for Niels Madsen Blyeberg, buried 20 December 1802, Stagstrup, Thisted, Denmark.

## Father of Mikkel Nielsen Blyeberg (baptized 20 August 1779, Stagstrup, Thisted Amt, Denmark)

**Conclusion (Probable):** The father of Mikkel Nielsen Blyeberg was **Niels Blyeberg**, whose full name was **Niels Madsen Blyeberg**. The conclusion rests on one strong contemporaneous paternity source corroborated by three additional sources that establish identity; it is classified *probable* rather than *proved* because all searched sources are derivative databases and original parish register images were not accessed.

### Evidence

**1. Baptismal record — direct evidence (primary information)**
The “Denmark Baptisms, 1618–1923” database on FamilySearch (derivative, col. 1778463) records the christening of *Michel* on 20 August 1779 at Stagstrup, Thisted, Denmark, naming *Niels Blyeberg* as father (ark:/61903/1:1:H26C-HMW2).¹ The date and place exactly match the research subject’s known birth data. The name *Michel* is the standard 18th-century Latin/German form of *Mikkel*. The recording clergyman, present as a witness at the baptism, provided primary information for the baptism date and the father’s name. **This is the single strongest piece of evidence — a contemporaneous, witness-recorded, primary-information paternity assertion.**

**2. Marriage record — indirect corroboration (primary information)**
The “Denmark, Marriages, 1635–1916” database (derivative, col. 1520592) records the marriage of *Niels Madsen Blyeberg* to *Inger Michelsdatter* on 3 January 1778 at Stagstrup, Thisted (ark:/61903/1:1:FVR3-287).² This marriage occurred eighteen months before Mikkel’s baptism. This record does not mention Mikkel and does not assert paternity; it **establishes identity** — that a man named Niels Madsen Blyeberg was present in Stagstrup parish, married to Mikkel’s mother, at the right time. The bride’s surname *Michelsdatter* also explains the son’s name Mikkel (Danish naming conventions). Both parties provided primary information about their own identities.

**3. Probate index — corroborating identity evidence (indeterminate information)**
The “Denmark, Probate Indexes, 1674–1851” database (derivative, col. 2026233) records a probate proceeding in 1803 for *Niels Madsen Bliberg*, residence “Sundy Stagstrup,” with widow *Inger Mikkelsdatter* (ark:/61903/1:1:Q2WL-6QST).³ This record does not mention Mikkel and does not assert paternity; it **corroborates identity** — that the same Niels (Madsen) Blyeberg who married Inger in 1778 died ca. 1802–1803, consistent with the man whose child Mikkel was baptized in 1779. The informant for name fields is unknown.

**4. Burial record of Mikkel — corroborating paternity evidence (indeterminate information)**
The “Denmark Burials, 1640–1917” database (derivative, col. 1520593) records the burial of *Mikkel Nielsen Bliberg* on 18 July 1862 at Stagstrup, with *Niels Bliberg* listed in a ParentChild relationship as Mikkel’s father (ark:/61903/1:1:FVRC-V2F).⁴ **This is the second paternity-asserting source**, but it is substantially weaker than the 1779 baptism: the informant is unknown, the information quality is indeterminate, and the record post-dates Niels’s death by sixty years. It is consistent with the 1779 baptism but cannot corroborate it with the independence a same-era primary source would provide.

### Assessment and Limitations

**Evidentiary weight by function:** Two sources directly assert the parent-child relationship: the 1779 baptism (primary, contemporaneous, strongest) and Mikkel’s 1862 burial (indeterminate, 60-year gap, weak). The 1778 marriage and 1803 probate corroborate the *identity* of Niels Blyeberg in Stagstrup — confirming he was the right man in the right place at the right time, married to the right woman — but neither record mentions Mikkel or asserts fatherhood. The probative weight of this conclusion rests primarily on the 1779 baptism; the remaining sources lend circumstantial identity and timeline support.

No source names a different father. Name variants (Blyeberg/Bliberg; Michelsdatter/Mikkelsdatter) are routine Danish orthographic equivalents. No alternative candidate for a “Niels Blyeberg” at Stagstrup in the 1770s has been identified; the farm-name character of the surname makes confusion with an unrelated individual unlikely, though a systematic search for other men of that name in the parish was not conducted.

**Limitations:** All searched sources are derivative indexes. Original Stagstrup parish register images for 1750–1815 were not found via FamilySearch volume_search. The 1787 Danish census (not indexed on FamilySearch) and Hassing herred lægdsruller (not in indexed collection 2243379) were searched but are not accessible in this repository; the Danish national archive’s Arkivalieronline may hold originals. Access to the original baptismal register entry and a second independently-sourced, primary-information paternity assertion would advance the tier to *proved*.

### Footnotes
¹ “Denmark Baptisms, 1618–1923,” database, FamilySearch (https://familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:2:WDTL-3PT2 : accessed 9 July 2026), entry for Michel, baptized 20 August 1779, Stagstrup, Thisted, Denmark.
² “Denmark, Marriages, 1635–1916,” database, FamilySearch (https://familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:2:MGY3-GQN : accessed 9 July 2026), entry for Niels Madsen Blyeberg and Inger Michelsdatter, married 3 January 1778, Stagstrup, Thisted, Denmark.
³ “Denmark, Probate Indexes, 1674–1851,” database, FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:2:QL3F-FM7C : accessed 9 July 2026), entry for Niels Madsen Bliberg and Inger Mikkelsdatter, 1803; residence Sundy Stagstrup.
⁴ “Denmark Burials, 1640–1917,” database, FamilySearch (https://familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:2:1W4H-V3Z : 20 February 2020), entry for Mikkel Nielsen Bliberg, buried 18 July 1862, Stagstrup, Thisted, Denmark.
